questions.questionText: 1. Django is based on which framework?
questions.explanation: Django uses the MVT (Model-View-Template) pattern. Models handle data and database access, Views contain request-handling logic, Templates render HTML. This is Django's documented architecture, distinct from classic MVC or MVVM.

questions.questionText: 2. For a model to be visible in the admin interface it has to be registered in which file?
questions.explanation: The Django admin site displays models registered in the app's `admin.py` via `admin.site.register(MyModel)` or a `@admin.register` decorator. `settings.py` configures the project; `views.py` handles public URLs — neither registers admin models.

questions.questionText: 3. Which of the following statement(s) is true about the JavaScript?

options.content: It is a scripting language used to make the website interactive
It is an advanced version of Java for Desktop and Mobile application development
It is a markup language of Java to develop the webpages

questions.explanation: JavaScript is a lightweight, interpreted scripting language primarily known for adding interactivity to web pages. It is entirely distinct from Java.

questions.questionText: 4. To perform programming logic in Django templates, you can use 'template tags', what is a correct syntax to start a 'for' loop?
questions.explanation: Django template logic uses tags wrapped in `{% %}`. A for loop starts with `{% for x in y %}` and ends with `{% endfor %}`. Parentheses `(for x in y)` are invalid. `<% %>` belongs to other template engines, not Django.

questions.questionText: 5. What is a correct syntax for creating a Django project?
questions.explanation: The canonical command is `django-admin startproject my_tennis_club`, which scaffolds the project directory and inner config package. `django start` and `manage.py start-django` are not real commands. After creation, use `manage.py` inside the project for runserver, migrate, etc.

questions.questionText: 6. If you have a Django application named members, and a model named Member, what is a correct syntax to import the model?
questions.explanation: Django models live in `appname/models.py`. Standard import: `from members.models import Member`. JavaScript-style `import Member from members` and dotted `import members.Member` are not valid Python import syntax for this case.

questions.questionText: 7. Which is the correct syntax to call an external JavaScript file in the current HTML document?
questions.explanation: External JavaScript files are linked using the HTML `<script>` tag with the `src` (source) attribute pointing to the file path.

questions.questionText: 8. Which JavaScript method is used to access an HTML element by id?
questions.explanation: While 'a' represents the raw method name, 'c' explicitly shows the method signature with the required parameter, which is typically the expected answer in academic contexts. Technically, it is a method of the `document` object.

questions.questionText: 9. Which property is used to define the HTML content to an HTML element with a specific id?
questions.explanation: The `innerHTML` property sets or returns the HTML content inside an element.

questions.questionText: 10. When you have done changes in a model in Django, which command has to be executed in order to make the changes take effect in the database?
questions.explanation: To apply model changes to the database you run `python manage.py migrate`. That command is not listed. `makemigrations` (option C) only creates migration files — it does not alter the database. `runmigrations` and `executemigrations` are fake commands. Therefore D (None of the previous) is correct. Exam trap: students pick C because it is the only real Django command shown, but it is the wrong step for "take effect in the database."

questions.questionText: 11. Which JavaScript method is used to write on browser's console?
questions.explanation: `console.log()` is the standard JavaScript method used to print information to the web browser's developer console for debugging.

questions.questionText: 12. Which JavaScript method is used to write into an alert box?
questions.explanation: `window.alert()` (or simply `alert()`) instructs the browser to display a dialog box with an optional message, and waits until the user dismisses it.

questions.questionText: 13. In HTML DOM, which of the following properties are read-only?
questions.explanation: The `nodeName` property is strictly read-only (it returns the tag name like 'DIV' or '#text'). The `nodeValue` property is read-write for Text, Comment, and CDATA nodes.

questions.questionText: 14. Which is the correct syntax to declare a constant in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: When declaring a constant using the `const` keyword, you must initialize it with a value at the time of declaration.
Attempting to reassign a new value to a constant variable declared with `const` throws a `TypeError: Assignment to constant variable.`

questions.questionText: 16. Can we use a function as a variable value?
questions.explanation: Yes. In JavaScript, functions are first-class citizens, meaning they can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.
JavaScript is case-sensitive. `X` (uppercase) is the global variable `10`; `x` (lowercase) is a separate `let` inside `myfunc`, which is never called. `document.write(X)` outputs `10`.
`10 + 20` is `30`, then `30 + "5"` concatenates to `"305"`.
JavaScript only has one numerical type: `number`. It does not differentiate between integers and floats in the `typeof` evaluation.

questions.questionText: 20. Which is the correct syntax to access an object property in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Bracket notation with a string literal — `objectName["propertyName"]` — reads a property in JavaScript. Dot notation (`objectName.propertyName`) works too but is not listed. Option A (`objectName:propertyName`) is object-creation syntax, not access. Option B (`objectName[propertyName]`) only works when `propertyName` is a variable holding the key; without that context, C is correct.
The `reduce` function starts with an initial value of 2. It iterates through the array: 2+1=3, 3+2=5, 5+3=8, 8+4=12. Since 12 is not listed in options A, B, or C, the correct choice is D.
`reduce` with subtraction gives `170 - 50 - 25 = 95`.

questions.questionText: 23. "Expires: Tuesday, 17 Dec 2020 18:30 GMT" is a header denoting
questions.explanation: The `Expires` header is sent in an HTTP response and gives a date/time after which the cached response should be considered stale — time-based caching. It is not a request header and not about content-type. Pair: response + time-based caching.
`forEach` calls `myFunction` for each element. Each call adds the value to `result` (`result += value`), so the total is 10 + 20 + 30 = 60. The output is `Result: 60`.

The `map` function creates a new array by executing the provided function on each element. It squares each value, returning `[100, 400, 900]`. When an array is coerced to a string for `document.write`, it joins elements with commas, yielding `Result: 100,400,900`.

questions.questionText: 26. Which function is used to serialize an object into a JSON string in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The correct global object method is `JSON.stringify()`. (Option C specifies `stringify()`, which is the core method name).

questions.questionText: 27. In Python, ____ defines a block of statements.
questions.explanation: After a header line ending with a colon (`if`, `for`, `while`, `def`, `else`, etc.), Python expects an indented block on the following lines. There are no `{}` block delimiters. Consistent leading whitespace (usually four spaces) groups statements into one block; dedenting ends the block. Option A (`Block`) and B (`Loop`) name concepts, not Python's block syntax. Getting indentation wrong raises `IndentationError` before the program runs.
The `find()` method returns the value of the *first* element in the provided array that satisfies the testing function. The first value greater than 10 is 20.
`cars.company` is `undefined` after delete; `cars["color"]` is `"white"`.
`eval("x * y")` returns the number `200`, not the string `"200"`.
`filter()` returns an array; `typeof` an array is `"object"`.
`lastIndexOf(10)` is 2, `indexOf(30)` is 3, sum is 5.

questions.questionText:
33. What will be the output of the following JavaScript code snippet?
document.write(typeof(NaN));
questions.explanation: In JavaScript, `typeof NaN` evaluates to `"number"` because `NaN` is a numeric value.

`concat` merges the array with the new elements, creating `[1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 8]`. When `document.write` coerces this array to a string for display, it outputs the elements separated by commas: `1,2,3,6,7,8`.
The semicolon after the `for` loop leaves an empty body; `document.write(a)` runs once and prints `5`.

questions.questionText:
36. What will be the output of the following Python code?
newtuple = (1, 2, 3, 4)
(*k, ) = newtuple
print(k)
questions.explanation: The assignment `(*k,) = (1, 2, 3, 4)` uses starred unpacking on the left. Because there is only one target (`k`) with a star, it receives every element of the tuple as a list: `[1, 2, 3, 4]`. The trailing comma in `(*k,)` is required — without it, parentheses would be grouping, not a valid one-target unpack pattern. `print(k)` therefore shows `[1, 2, 3, 4]`. Options A and C list partial values without brackets; they describe different unpack shapes.

questions.questionText:
37. What will be the output of the following Python code?
newtuple = (1, 2, 3, 4)
(x, *k, y) = newtuple
print(k)
questions.explanation: Tuple unpacking assigns by position. In `(x, *k, y) = (1, 2, 3, 4)`, `x` takes the first value `1`, `y` takes the last value `4`, and the starred name `k` absorbs the middle values as a list. Therefore `k == [2, 3]`. Starred targets always collect a list (possibly empty) of leftover items. Option A shows bare values without list brackets. Option B incorrectly includes the final `4` in `k`.

questions.questionText: 38. Which is the correct HTML statement to define the red color of the paragraph text?
questions.explanation: CSS allows color definition using predefined color names (like "red") or hexadecimal codes (like "#ff0000"). Both syntaxes are valid.

questions.questionText: 39. Which HTML tag is used to define highlighted text?
questions.explanation: The `<mark>` tag is the standard HTML5 element used to represent text that has been highlighted or marked for reference purposes.

questions.questionText: 40. Which is the correct HTML statement to display H2O in a paragraph?
questions.explanation:
H₂O needs `` for the 2; option B is `

H2O

`.

questions.questionText: 41. Which is the correct HTML statement to display <del>Hello</del> <u>Hi</u> in a paragraph?
questions.explanation: Use `<del>` for deleted text and `<ins>` for inserted text; `<cut>` is not valid HTML.

questions.questionText: 42. Which attribute specifies the path to the image?
questions.explanation: The `` tag uses the `src` attribute to specify the image path.

questions.questionText: 43. Can we use class name with multiple HTML elements?
questions.explanation: Yes. Unlike the `id` attribute, which must be unique per document, `class` attributes are specifically designed to be reused across multiple elements.

questions.questionText: 44. The ____ property is used in the positioning of the background image.
questions.explanation: The `background-position` CSS property sets the initial position for a background image.

questions.questionText: 45. Which of the following selectors selects the specified elements of type E with a particular class value?
questions.explanation: The CSS syntax for selecting an element `E` that possesses a specific class name is `E.classname`.

questions.questionText: 46. Which of the following selectors selects all elements of E that have the attribute (attr) that ends with a given (value)?
questions.explanation: The `$=` operator in a CSS attribute selector strictly matches elements whose attribute value ends with the specified string.

questions.questionText: 47. Which of the following selectors selects an element that has no children?
questions.explanation: The `:empty` pseudo-class represents any element that has no children (including text nodes).

questions.questionText:
48. What will be the output of the following Python code?
b = "Hello, World!"
print(b[-5:-2])
questions.explanation: Negative indices count from the end: index `-1` is `'!'`, `-2` is `'d'`, `-3` is `'l'`, `-4` is `'r'`, `-5` is `'o'`. Slice `b[-5:-2]` starts at `'o'` and stops before index `-2` (`'d'`), so the slice characters are `'o'`, `'r'`, `'l'` → `orl`. Slices exclude the stop index. Option B (`or`) would use stop `-3`. Option C (`Wor`) uses positive-index thinking and is out of range for this negative slice.

questions.questionText:
49. What will be the output of the following Python code?
thistuple = (1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5)
x = thistuple.index(3)
print(x)
questions.explanation: `tuple.index(value)` returns the zero-based index of the first matching element, not the value itself and not the count of occurrences. The first `3` in `(1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5)` sits at index `2`, so `print(x)` outputs `2`. Option A (`3`) confuses index with value. Option B (`4`) might come from counting duplicates or using 1-based indexing.

questions.questionText: 50. If an application is running on Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface (ASGI), then it will handle client requests in a sequential manner?
questions.explanation: ASGI (Asynchronous Server Gateway Interface) supports async views and concurrent request handling within an event loop. WSGI is synchronous — each worker typically handles one request at a time in sequence. The statement claims ASGI is sequential, which is false.

questions.questionText: 51. Can you play audios in HTML?
questions.explanation: Yes, HTML5 introduced the native `<audio>` element for embedding sound content.

questions.questionText:
52. What will be the output of the following Python code?
fruits = {"apple", "banana", "cherry"}
fruits.add("apple")
x = len(fruits)
print(x)
questions.explanation: Sets store unique elements only. `fruits` begins with three distinct strings. `fruits.add("apple")` attempts to insert a duplicate; sets ignore duplicates, so membership and length stay unchanged. `len(fruits)` remains `3`. Option A assumes duplicates increase length. Sets are unordered, but length always reflects unique members.

questions.questionText:
53. What will be the output of the following Python code?
car = {
"brand": "Ford",
"model": "Mustang",
"year": 1964
}
y = car.keys()
car["color"] = "red"
print(len(y))
questions.explanation: `dict.keys()` returns a dynamic view object, not a static snapshot. Variable `y` stays linked to the dictionary's keys, so when `car["color"] = "red"` adds a fourth key, `len(y)` becomes `4` even though `y` was created when there were only three keys. If `keys()` returned a copy, the length would remain `3`. This live-view behavior is a common exam trap.

questions.questionText: 54. To add items from another set to the current set we use ____ method, while to append items from another list to the current list we use ____ method.
questions.explanation: Adding *multiple items from another collection* uses different methods per type. Sets merge elements with `update(other_set)` (or `|=`); duplicates are ignored automatically. Lists append each element from an iterable with `extend(other_list)`. `append()` only adds one object as a single element — `lst.append(other_list)` would nest the whole list, not merge items. Option A swaps in `append()` for the list side; B and D pair `extend()` with sets, which lists do not support.

questions.questionText: 55. To make a copy of a dictionary, we can use ____ method.
questions.explanation: Both `my_dict.copy()` and `dict(my_dict)` create a new shallow copy of the dictionary — new outer dict, but nested objects are shared if present. Either works for a flat dict copy, so option C ("a. and b.") is correct. `makecopy()` is not a real dict method. Exam trap: shallow copy is enough for the question; `copy.deepcopy()` is only needed for nested mutable values inside values.

questions.questionText: 56. In CSS, "color:red" can be called as
questions.explanation: In CSS terminology, the combination of a property (`color`) and its value (`red`) separated by a colon is formally known as a Declaration.

questions.questionText: 57. External CSS style has the highest priority
questions.explanation: False. Inline styles (using the `style` attribute directly on an HTML element) have the highest specificity and priority, overriding external stylesheets.

questions.questionText: 58. The ____ HTTP request header specifies information about the software (browser) making the request.
questions.explanation: The `User-Agent` request header identifies the client software (browser, version, OS). `Referer` (sic in HTTP spec) sends the previous page URL. `Cookie` sends stored cookies. Servers use User-Agent for analytics and compatibility decisions.

questions.questionText: 59. What does `{{ name }}` mean in Django Templates?

options.content: It will be displayed as name in HTML.
The name will be replaced with values of Python variable.

questions.explanation: `{{ name }}` in a Django template renders the value of the context variable `name` at that position. It is variable interpolation, not literal text. The view must pass `name` in the context dict. Spacing inside `{{ name }}` is conventional; `{{name}}` also works but option C describes the semantics correctly.
With string operands, `+` concatenates to `2010`. The `-` operator coerces the strings to numbers, giving `10`.

questions.questionText: 61. Which of the following is the correct way of creating a hyperlink in HTML?
questions.explanation: The proper standard structure for a hyperlink utilizes the `` (anchor) tag, assigning the URL destination to the `href` attribute, and wrapping the clickable text before the `` closing tag.

questions.questionText: 62. What is the purpose of using div tags in HTML?
questions.explanation: `<div>` is a structural container for grouping sections, not for styling, headings, or titles by itself.

questions.questionText: 63. Which of the following is the correct way to add background color in HTML?
questions.explanation: Inline CSS is the correct modern approach: use the `style` attribute with `property: value` pairs. Options c and d are both allowable HTML/CSS; a trailing semicolon after the last declaration is optional, but including one is preferred (as on lecture slide 14, Inline CSS).

questions.questionText: 64. If you have the following JavaScript statement: const f = (a, b) => a + b + 5;

options.content: Not a correct syntax for an arrow function

questions.explanation: Valid arrow syntax; `f(4, 5)` returns `14`.

questions.questionText: 65. To multiply an array of numbers (named myNumbers) with 4, the following is a correct syntax to do it:
questions.explanation: The `map()` method transforms every element and requires a callback function. Option A supplies a valid arrow function.

questions.questionText: 66. To keep numbers that are less than 10 from an array of numbers (named myNumbers), the following is a correct syntax to do it:
questions.explanation: The `filter()` method keeps elements that pass a callback test. Option B supplies a valid arrow function.

questions.questionText: 67. var colors = {1:"red", 2:"green", 3: "blue"} is one of the ways of defining an array in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: This syntax is invalid. Arrays are defined using square brackets `[]` (e.g., `["red", "green", "blue"]`).

questions.questionText:
68. In the following statement: myNumbers = [1, 2, 3]; x = myNumbers.pop();
The removed item will be 1
questions.explanation: For a Python list, `pop()` with no index removes and returns the last element. `[1, 2, 3]` ends with `3`, not `1`. The statement says the removed item will be `1`, which is false. To remove the first element you must call `pop(0)`. Exam trick: do not confuse list `pop()` with `set.pop()`, which removes an arbitrary element.

questions.questionText:
69. In the following statement: myNumbers = [1, 2, 3]; x = myNumbers.pop();
The removed item will be 3
questions.explanation: For a Python list, `pop()` with no argument removes the final element. In `[1, 2, 3]`, the last item is `3`, so `x = myNumbers.pop()` sets `x` to `3` and leaves `[1, 2]`. The statement is therefore true. This pairs with question 68, which tests the same method but with a wrong claimed value.

questions.questionText: 70. In Python, the value of [] is False?
questions.explanation: Empty built-in containers are falsy in boolean context. `bool([])` is `False`, as are `bool({})`, `bool(set())`, and `bool("")`. Any list with at least one element is truthy, even `[0]` or `[False]`. The statement is true. This pairs with set/index questions: `[]` is a valid list you can call methods on — falsy does not mean "invalid" or "error".

questions.questionText: 71. In Python, it is possible to use the set() constructor to create a set?
questions.explanation: Python provides the built-in `set()` constructor. You can build sets from iterables (`set([1, 2, 3])`), strings (`set("abc")`), or empty sets (`set()`). Curly-brace literals like `{1, 2, 3}` are another syntax, not the only way. The statement is true. Note: `{}` alone creates an empty dict, not a set — use `set()` for an empty set.

questions.questionText: 72. In Python, A set is a collection which is both unordered and indexed?
questions.explanation: Sets store unique elements without positional indexing. You cannot do `s[0]` or iterate by index; use a `for` loop or convert to `list(s)` if order matters. Sets are unordered (iteration order is not a reliable indexing scheme). The statement incorrectly adds "indexed", so it is false. Contrast 2025 Q18/Q7, which state the correct "unordered and unindexed" definition.

questions.questionText:
73. def f(**person):
print("Person's name is " + person["name"])
f(name="Ahmed", age=10)
The above statement will return:

options.content: Person's name is Ahmed and Age is 10
Error as the function f doesn't take except one argument
Error as the function f doesn't take an argument called "name"

questions.explanation: The `**person` syntax gathers keyword arguments into a dictionary inside the function. `f(name="Ahmed", age=10)` makes `person` equivalent to `{"name": "Ahmed", "age": 10}`. Only `person["name"]` is used in the `print`, so output is `Person's name is Ahmed`. Extra keys like `age` are valid and do not cause an error. Options C and D incorrectly suggest keyword arguments break the definition.

questions.questionText:
74. y = lambda x : x / 20
print(y(100))
The above statement will return:

options.content: Error as there is no function called y

questions.explanation: `lambda x: x / 20` divides using `/`, which in Python 3 always produces a float. `100 / 20` evaluates to `5.0`, and `print` displays `5.0`. Option C (`5`) is the integer-looking value but is not what `/` returns. Option B shows source code, not evaluated output. Use `//` for floor division if you need integer `5`.

questions.questionText:
75. In the following Python statements:
if 1 < 2:
print("One is less than Two!")
print("One is less than Two!")

options.content: The output will be: One is less than Two! One is less than Two!
The output will be: One is less than Two!

The output will be: One is less than Two!
One is less than Two!
The output will be an Error

questions.explanation: Python uses indentation to define blocks. After `if 1 < 2:`, the next line must be indented to belong to the `if` body. Here the first `print` is not indented relative to the `if`, so Python raises `IndentationError` at parse time — no output is produced. Options A–C describe runtime output that cannot occur because the file fails before execution. Compare with 2021 Q58, where indentation is valid and the second `print` sits outside the `if`.

questions.questionText:
76. The output of the following statement in python is:
x = "My Lovely Bird!"
print(x[:7])
questions.explanation: Slice `x[:7]` includes indices `0` through `6` (the stop `7` is excluded). String `"My Lovely Bird!"` indices: `0:M, 1:y, 2:space, 3:L, 4:o, 5:v, 6:e`. Result: `"My Love"` (seven characters). Option B (`My Lovel`) would include index `7` (`l` from `Lovely`). Option C (`ly Bird!`) is the suffix starting at index `7`, not the prefix.

questions.questionText:
77. The output of the following statement in python is:
x = "My Lovely Bird!"
print(x[7:])
questions.explanation: Slice `x[7:]` starts at index `7` and runs to the end. Index `7` is the first `l` in `Lovely`, so the slice is `"ly Bird!"` (from that `l` through the exclamation mark). Option D (`y Bird!`) would start one index too late. Option A (`My Love`) is the `[:7]` prefix, not the suffix tested here.
With depth 1, `flat()` yields `[1, 2, 3, [4, 5, 6], 7, 8]`. `document.writeln` coerces the array to a string; nested arrays join with commas, producing `1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8`.

context.text: Based on the following HTML page, answer the following questions:
context.code:
Hello World

Welcome

Paragraph 1.

Paragraph 2.

Paragraph 3.

Paragraph 4.

Paragraph 5.

questions.questionText: 79. To get the value of the text node of the h2 element node, you can use
questions.explanation: Text inside an element lives in a child text node. The correct approach is `document.getElementById("id1").firstChild.nodeValue`. `innerHTML` is not equivalent; `.text` is not a standard element property; and `nodeValue` on the element itself is `null`.

questions.questionText: 80. Based on the above html page, the content of h2 element will be presented as
questions.explanation:
The script sets `h2` innerHTML to `my.length`, the count of `

` elements (6).

questions.questionText: 81. The number of objects in my variable is
questions.explanation:
Six `

` elements exist in the page, so the collection length is 6.

questions.questionText: 82. By using this CSS selector div > p { color: red; }, how many p elements will be styled?
questions.explanation:
The child combinator `>` selects only direct `

` children of `

`: Paragraph 1 and Paragraph 2. Paragraph 3 is inside `
`, not a direct child of `
`.

questions.questionText: 83. By using this CSS selector div ~ p { color: blue; }, the number of p elements that will be styled is
questions.explanation:
The general sibling combinator `~` selects `

` siblings that follow `

` at the same level: Paragraph 4 and Paragraph 5.

questions.questionText: 84. Using document.getElementById("id1").nodeType will return
questions.explanation: The `nodeType` property returns an integer. For an element node (like the `<h2>` targeted here), `nodeType` is `1`.

topic: CSS Selectors and HTML Parsing - Snippet 2

context.text: Based on the following HTML page, answer the following questions
context.code:
CSS Selector

Hello I am Here: P1

Hello I am Here: Paragraph 2

Hello I am Here: P3

w3schools.com disney.com wikipedia.org

questions.questionText: 85. In a[target=_blank], the [target=_blank] is called
questions.explanation: Square brackets `[]` encapsulate CSS attribute selectors, identifying components by the presence or explicit value of HTML attributes.

questions.questionText: 86. By using p:first-child { color:red; }, the number of elements that will be styled
questions.explanation:
`:first-child` matches only when an element is the first child of its parent. Under ``, `

` is the first child, not any `

`, so none match.

questions.questionText: 87. To change the background color of all <a> elements to be yellow, you can use
questions.explanation: Using just the plain element selector (`a`) targets all anchor tags ubiquitously across the document.

questions.questionText: 88. To change the color of <a> elements to yellow when the mouse moves over it, you can use
questions.explanation: The `:hover` pseudo-class specifically manages state changes triggered by a user placing their pointing device over an element.

questions.questionText: 89. href is a/an ____ while "_blank" is a ____
questions.explanation: In HTML syntax rules, `href` acts as the property name (the attribute), whereas strings enclosed in quotes like `"_blank"` operate as assigned values.

questions.questionText: 90. The hyperlink ... is an example of a/an ____ URL, while ... is an example of a/an ____ URL.
questions.explanation: An Absolute URL includes the entire operational path (protocol and domain). A Relative URL merely provides the path correlating to the currently loaded page's domain/directory.
